title: Design a URL Shortener

stages.prompt: Design a URL shortener (like bit.ly) that serves 300,000 requests per minute. What infrastructure, classes, and data model would you use?
stages.answer: Core API: POST /shorten (long_url) -> short_code, GET /{short_code} -> 302 redirect. Generate short codes with a base62 counter (or hash + collision check) stored in a key-value table {short_code, long_url, created_at, expires_at, owner_id}. 300K req/min (~5K req/s) is mostly read-heavy (redirects >> creations), so put a CDN/edge cache or Redis cache in front of a relational or key-value store (DynamoDB/Cassandra) keyed by short_code. Use a load balancer -> stateless API servers -> cache -> database. Rate-limit the write path, and use a separate counter/ID-generation service (e.g. Snowflake-style IDs or a pre-allocated block of IDs per server) to avoid a single point of contention when minting new codes.

stages.prompt: Traffic suddenly grows to 4,000,000 requests per minute (~66K req/s). What has to change? How does this affect your storage and database choices?
stages.answer: At ~66K req/s reads, a single database (even read-replicas) becomes a bottleneck, so lean harder on caching: a multi-layer cache (CDN edge caching for redirects, then a distributed in-memory cache like Redis/Memcached cluster) should absorb the vast majority of traffic since URL access is heavily skewed (Pareto - a small % of links get most clicks). Move from a single relational DB to a horizontally-partitioned/sharded key-value store (DynamoDB, Cassandra) sharded by short_code hash, so no single node is a hotspot. Writes (new short URLs) stay comparatively low volume, so a single write path with async replication is fine. Add read-replicas per shard, and consider consistent hashing so adding shards doesn't require a full re-shard. Also introduce circuit breakers and autoscaling for API servers behind the load balancer.

stages.prompt: What managed/out-of-the-box cloud services would you use to implement this end-to-end?
stages.answer: AWS example: CloudFront (CDN/edge cache) -> API Gateway or an ALB in front of stateless services on ECS/EKS or Lambda -> DynamoDB (auto-sharded, single-digit ms latency) with DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX) as an in-memory cache layer -> S3 + Athena for click-analytics logs -> Route 53 for DNS -> CloudWatch for monitoring/alerting and auto-scaling triggers. Equivalent GCP stack: Cloud CDN, Cloud Load Balancing, Cloud Run/GKE, Firestore/Bigtable, Memorystore (Redis), BigQuery for analytics.

diagram:
flowchart LR
    Client -->|GET short_code| CDN[CDN / Edge Cache]
    CDN -->|cache miss| LB[Load Balancer]
    LB --> API1[API Server]
    LB --> API2[API Server]
    API1 --> Cache[(Redis Cache)]
    API2 --> Cache
    Cache -->|cache miss| DB[(Sharded KV Store\nDynamoDB/Cassandra)]
    API1 --> IDGen[ID Generator\nSnowflake-style]
    IDGen --> DB

title: Design a Cloud Image/File Storage System

stages.prompt: Design a cloud image storage system (like Google Photos or S3) supporting uploads, downloads, and metadata search for millions of users.
stages.answer: Separate metadata from blob storage. Metadata service (relational or document DB) stores {file_id, owner_id, filename, size, content_type, storage_path, thumbnails, created_at, tags}. Actual bytes go to an object store (blob storage split into shards/buckets by hash of file_id) designed for large binary objects with high durability (replicate 3x across availability zones). Upload flow: client requests a pre-signed upload URL from the metadata/API service, uploads directly to object storage (bypassing the app server for the large payload), then confirms completion so metadata is written. Downloads: served via CDN using signed URLs. A background worker generates thumbnails/transcodes and updates metadata asynchronously via a queue.

stages.prompt: How would you scale this to support 500 million users uploading billions of images, while keeping search over metadata (like 'photos from last summer' or tags) fast?
stages.answer: Shard the object store by consistent hashing on file_id/user_id to spread load evenly, and use erasure coding or replication for durability at that scale. For metadata, a single relational DB won't scale to billions of rows with flexible search; move to a combination of a sharded document store (partitioned by user_id, since most queries are per-user) plus a dedicated search index (Elasticsearch/OpenSearch) for tag/date/full-text search, updated asynchronously via change-data-capture from the metadata store. Use a message queue (Kafka) to decouple upload confirmation from thumbnail generation, ML-based tagging, and search-index updates so the write path stays fast. Cache 'hot' metadata (recent photos, popular shares) in Redis.

stages.prompt: What managed cloud services would map to this architecture?
stages.answer: AWS: S3 for blob storage (built-in multi-AZ replication and lifecycle tiers like S3 Glacier for cold storage), S3 pre-signed URLs for direct client upload, DynamoDB or Aurora (sharded) for metadata, OpenSearch Service for tag/date search, SQS/Kafka (MSK) for the async processing queue, Lambda or ECS workers for thumbnail generation, Rekognition for automatic tagging, and CloudFront for downloads. Equivalent: GCS + Firestore/Cloud SQL + Cloud Search + Pub/Sub + Cloud Functions + Vision AI + Cloud CDN.

diagram:
flowchart TD
    Client -->|1. request upload URL| API[Metadata API]
    API -->|2. pre-signed URL| Client
    Client -->|3. upload bytes| Blob[(Object Storage\nS3/GCS, sharded)]
    Client -->|4. confirm upload| API
    API --> MetaDB[(Metadata DB\nsharded by user_id)]
    API --> Queue[(Message Queue)]
    Queue --> Worker[Thumbnail/Tagging Worker]
    Worker --> Blob
    Worker --> SearchIdx[(Search Index)]
    Client -->|search| SearchIdx
    Client -->|download| CDN[CDN] --> Blob

title: Design a Rate Limiter

stages.prompt: Design a rate limiter that can be applied to an API gateway to limit clients to N requests per minute.
stages.answer: Use the token bucket or sliding-window-counter algorithm. Store per-client counters in a fast in-memory store (Redis) keyed by client_id + time window, using atomic INCR with an expiry (TTL) equal to the window size. On each request, increment the counter; if it exceeds the limit, reject with HTTP 429. Token bucket is preferred when you want to allow short bursts: each client has a bucket with a max capacity and a refill rate; a request consumes a token if available.

stages.prompt: How do you make this rate limiter work correctly across many API gateway instances (distributed), without a shared Redis becoming a bottleneck or single point of failure?
stages.answer: Use a Redis cluster (sharded by client_id) rather than a single instance, with replicas for failover. To cut network round-trips, use local in-memory approximate counters on each gateway node that sync to Redis periodically (or use Redis Lua scripts to make the check-and-increment atomic in one round trip). For very high scale, consider a sliding-window-log approach approximated with fixed windows to bound memory, and accept slight over/under-counting at the edges as a tradeoff for performance. Add circuit breaking so if Redis is unreachable, the gateway fails open (allow traffic) or fails closed (block) based on the criticality of the service.

stages.prompt: What out-of-the-box services or API gateway features would you use instead of building this from scratch?
stages.answer: Most managed API gateways have this built in: AWS API Gateway usage plans/throttling, Amazon CloudFront + AWS WAF rate-based rules, Kong or Envoy rate-limiting plugins backed by Redis, or Google Cloud Armor / Apigee for GCP. For a custom microservice mesh, Envoy's global rate limit service (backed by Redis) is a common off-the-shelf building block.

diagram:
flowchart LR
    Client --> GW1[Gateway Node 1]
    Client --> GW2[Gateway Node 2]
    GW1 -->|INCR client_id, window| Redis[(Redis Cluster\nsharded counters)]
    GW2 -->|INCR client_id, window| Redis
    Redis -->|count > limit?| GW1
    GW1 -->|429 Too Many Requests| Client

title: Design a Chat/Messaging System

stages.prompt: Design a real-time chat system (like WhatsApp or Slack) supporting 1-on-1 and group messaging.
stages.answer: Clients hold a persistent WebSocket connection to a connection-gateway layer; each gateway server tracks which user_ids are connected to it. Sending a message: client -> gateway -> message service, which persists the message to a database (partitioned by conversation_id) and looks up which gateway server(s) the recipient(s) are connected to (via a connection-registry service, e.g. Redis mapping user_id -> gateway_node), then pushes the message over that recipient's WebSocket. If the recipient is offline, the message waits in their inbox/queue and a push notification is sent instead. Message ordering per conversation is preserved with a monotonically increasing sequence number per conversation.

stages.prompt: How do you scale this to hundreds of millions of concurrent users while keeping message delivery low-latency and ensuring no messages are lost if a gateway server crashes?
stages.answer: Shard the connection-registry (Redis) and message store by user_id/conversation_id across many nodes. Use a pub/sub backbone (Kafka) so the message service publishes to a topic per shard, and gateway servers subscribe to the shards containing their connected users, decoupling delivery from a direct service-to-service call. For crash recovery: since the message was already durably written to the database/Kafka before acknowledging the sender, a crashed gateway simply means the client reconnects (to a different gateway) and the connection-registry is updated; undelivered messages are re-sent from the persisted store when the client reconnects and requests messages since its last-seen sequence number. Use consistent hashing so gateway/registry rebalancing on scale-out is minimally disruptive.

stages.prompt: What managed cloud services would you use?
stages.answer: AWS: API Gateway WebSocket APIs or ALB with sticky sessions for the connection layer, DynamoDB (partitioned by conversation_id) for message storage, Kinesis or MSK (managed Kafka) for the pub/sub backbone, ElastiCache (Redis) for the connection registry, SNS/Amazon Pinpoint for push notifications to offline users. Alternatively, many teams use a managed pub/sub chat backend outright (e.g., AWS AppSync with GraphQL subscriptions, or Firebase Realtime Database/Firestore with its built-in listeners) to avoid building the WebSocket fan-out layer themselves.

diagram:
flowchart TD
    C1[Client A] <-->|WebSocket| GW1[Gateway Node 1]
    C2[Client B] <-->|WebSocket| GW2[Gateway Node 2]
    GW1 --> MsgSvc[Message Service]
    MsgSvc --> DB[(Message Store\nsharded by conversation_id)]
    MsgSvc --> Registry[(Connection Registry\nuser_id -> gateway_node)]
    MsgSvc --> Bus[(Pub/Sub - Kafka)]
    Bus --> GW2
    GW2 -->|push message| C2

title: Design a News Feed System

stages.prompt: Design a social media news feed (like Twitter/X or Instagram's feed) that shows a user posts from people they follow, ranked roughly by recency.
stages.answer: Two classic approaches: fan-out-on-write (push) and fan-out-on-read (pull). Push: when a user posts, write the post_id into every follower's precomputed feed list (stored in Redis/Cassandra as a per-user list). Reading the feed is then just an O(1) lookup - very fast for the common read-heavy case. Pull: store posts per author; on read, merge the latest posts from everyone the user follows on-the-fly (like a k-way merge). Push is fast to read but expensive to write for high-follower accounts (celebrities); pull is the opposite.

stages.prompt: How do you handle celebrity accounts with 50 million followers, where fan-out-on-write would mean 50 million writes per post?
stages.answer: Use a hybrid approach: for normal users, fan-out-on-write into follower feed lists as before. For celebrity/high-follower accounts (above some threshold), skip fan-out entirely and instead merge their recent posts into the requester's feed at read-time (pull), since celebrities are relatively few and their posts can be cached and merged on-demand. This bounds worst-case write amplification. Also cache the precomputed feed lists in Redis with a bounded length (e.g., last 500 items) and lazily backfill from the database on scroll (pagination) rather than storing unbounded history in the hot cache.

stages.prompt: What managed services and ranking considerations would you add for a production system?
stages.answer: Use a message queue (Kafka) to decouple 'post created' events from the fan-out workers so posting stays fast even if fan-out takes time. Store feed lists in a low-latency store like Redis/DynamoDB, posts themselves in a sharded database or object store (for media, S3 + CDN). For ranking beyond pure recency, a separate ranking/ML service scores candidate posts (engagement prediction) and can be added as a re-ranking step after retrieving candidates from the feed list, often using a feature store and a model-serving service (e.g., SageMaker, Vertex AI) - but the core retrieval architecture (hybrid push/pull) stays the same.

diagram:
flowchart TD
    Post[New Post by User] --> Queue[(Kafka: post-created)]
    Queue --> FanOut[Fan-out Worker]
    FanOut -->|normal user: push to followers| FeedStore[(Per-user Feed Lists\nRedis/Cassandra)]
    Celeb[Celebrity Post] -.->|skip fan-out| PullMerge[Read-time Merge]
    Client -->|GET feed| FeedSvc[Feed Service]
    FeedSvc --> FeedStore
    FeedSvc --> PullMerge
    FeedSvc --> Rank[Ranking Service]
    Rank --> Client

title: Design a Distributed Cache

stages.prompt: Design a distributed, in-memory caching layer (like a simplified Redis/Memcached cluster) that sits in front of a database.
stages.answer: Cache API: get(key), set(key, value, ttl), delete(key). Use consistent hashing to map keys to a set of cache nodes so that adding/removing nodes only reshuffles a small fraction of keys. On a read: check cache first (cache-aside pattern) - on a hit, return immediately; on a miss, read from the database, populate the cache, and return. On a write: either write-through (update cache and DB together) or invalidate the cache key so the next read repopulates it. Each entry has a TTL to bound staleness and memory usage; use an LRU/LFU eviction policy when a node is at capacity.

stages.prompt: How do you handle a 'hot key' problem, where one key (e.g., a viral post) receives a disproportionate share of traffic and overwhelms the single cache node responsible for it?
stages.answer: Detect hot keys (via sampling/monitoring request rates per key) and replicate that specific key across multiple cache nodes rather than relying on consistent hashing to route all its traffic to one node; the client or a routing layer picks a random replica for reads. Alternatively, add a small local (in-process) cache on each application server for the very hottest keys, so most reads never even reach the distributed cache. Also consider request coalescing: if many concurrent requests miss the cache for the same key simultaneously, only let one of them query the database (others wait on that in-flight result) to avoid a 'thundering herd' on the DB.

stages.prompt: What managed cloud offerings would you use instead of self-hosting this?
stages.answer: AWS ElastiCache (Redis or Memcached, managed clustering/sharding/replication built in), Google Cloud Memorystore, Azure Cache for Redis. These handle consistent hashing, failover, and monitoring for you; you'd typically only build custom logic for hot-key mitigation and cache-aside/invalidation patterns in your application layer on top of the managed service.

diagram:
flowchart LR
    App[App Server] -->|1. get key| Router[Consistent Hash Router]
    Router --> N1[(Cache Node 1)]
    Router --> N2[(Cache Node 2)]
    Router --> N3[(Cache Node 3)]
    App -->|2. miss -> read| DB[(Database)]
    App -->|3. populate cache| N2
    Hot[Hot Key Detector] -.->|replicate hot key| N1
    Hot -.-> N3

title: Design a Web Crawler

stages.prompt: Design a web crawler that can index a large portion of the public web.
stages.answer: Core components: a URL frontier (queue of URLs to crawl, prioritized and deduplicated), a fetcher pool (workers that download pages), a parser (extracts links and content), and a store (raw HTML + extracted text/metadata). Use a distributed queue (Kafka/SQS) for the frontier so many fetcher workers can pull URLs in parallel. Maintain a 'seen URL' set (bloom filter or hash set in a fast store) to avoid re-crawling the same URL. Respect robots.txt and apply per-domain rate limiting so the crawler doesn't overwhelm any single site.

stages.prompt: How do you scale this to billions of pages while being polite to individual domains (not hammering any one site) and avoiding duplicate crawls of near-identical content?
stages.answer: Partition the frontier by domain (e.g., consistent hashing on domain) so each domain's URLs are handled by a bounded set of workers, letting you enforce a per-domain politeness delay/rate limit centrally (a per-domain queue with its own throttle). Use a distributed bloom filter (or a sharded key-value store) for the 'seen URL' set to handle billions of URLs without one node holding all of them in memory. For near-duplicate detection, compute a content fingerprint (e.g., SimHash or MinHash) of each page and check it against previously seen fingerprints to skip near-identical pages (common with mirrors/URL parameters).

stages.prompt: What existing tools or cloud services would you leverage rather than building everything from scratch?
stages.answer: Use an existing crawler framework (Apache Nutch, Scrapy with a distributed scheduler like scrapy-redis) rather than writing a fetcher/parser from scratch. For infrastructure: SQS/Kafka for the frontier queue, S3 for raw page storage, DynamoDB or a Bloom-filter-backed Redis for URL dedup, Lambda/ECS for stateless fetcher workers, and Elasticsearch/OpenSearch for the resulting search index. Managed crawling APIs (e.g., Common Crawl's open dataset) can also be a starting point instead of crawling the entire web yourself.

diagram:
flowchart LR
    Frontier[(URL Frontier\npartitioned by domain)] --> W1[Fetcher Worker]
    Frontier --> W2[Fetcher Worker]
    W1 --> Parser[Parser]
    Parser -->|new links| Dedup{Seen URL?\nBloom Filter}
    Dedup -->|no| Frontier
    Parser --> Store[(Page Store - S3)]
    Parser --> Fingerprint[SimHash Dedup]
    Store --> Index[(Search Index)]

title: Design a Ride-Sharing Dispatch System

stages.prompt: Design the core system for matching riders with nearby drivers (like Uber/Lyft dispatch).
stages.answer: Drivers periodically send location updates (lat/lng) over a lightweight connection; store current driver locations in a geospatial index (e.g., a grid/geohash-based structure or Redis with GEO commands) so 'find drivers near X' is fast. When a rider requests a ride, query the geospatial index for nearby available drivers, rank by ETA (distance + traffic), and send a match request to the top candidate(s); the driver has a short window to accept before it's offered to the next. Store trip state (requested -> matched -> in-progress -> completed) in a trip service backed by a database keyed by trip_id.

stages.prompt: How do you handle a huge spike in demand in one area (e.g., a concert letting out) where supply of drivers is temporarily insufficient?
stages.answer: Introduce surge pricing computed per geographic cell (geohash region) based on the real-time ratio of open ride requests to available drivers in that cell, which both signals riders to expect a wait/pay more and incentivizes nearby drivers to reposition into the surge area. Widen the search radius for driver matching in undersupplied cells. Queue excess ride requests instead of failing them outright, with an estimated wait time shown to the rider. This all requires the geospatial index and matching service to be able to compute supply/demand ratios per cell in near real-time, typically via a streaming aggregation job (e.g., Kafka Streams/Flink) over location and request events.

stages.prompt: What managed cloud/geospatial services would you use?
stages.answer: Use a managed geospatial data store rather than building your own index: Redis with GEO commands (or Amazon ElastiCache for Redis), or a purpose-built geospatial database (MongoDB geospatial indexes, PostGIS on Aurora/RDS). For real-time location ingestion at scale, Kinesis or Kafka (MSK) streams driver pings into a stream-processing job (Kinesis Data Analytics/Flink) that maintains the live geospatial index and supply/demand aggregates. Google Maps Platform or Mapbox APIs provide routing/ETA calculations instead of building your own mapping engine.

diagram:
flowchart TD
    Driver[Driver App] -->|location ping| LocSvc[Location Ingest]
    LocSvc --> GeoIndex[(Geospatial Index\nRedis GEO / geohash grid)]
    Rider[Rider App] -->|request ride| MatchSvc[Matching Service]
    MatchSvc --> GeoIndex
    GeoIndex -->|nearby drivers| MatchSvc
    MatchSvc --> TripSvc[Trip Service]
    TripSvc --> DB[(Trip DB)]
    Stream[Streaming Aggregator] --> GeoIndex
    Stream --> Surge[Surge Pricing Calc]

title: Design a Notification System

stages.prompt: Design a notification system that can send push notifications, emails, and SMS to users based on events in your product (e.g., 'someone liked your post').
stages.answer: Producers (application services) publish notification events to a message queue rather than calling notification providers directly, decoupling business logic from delivery. A notification service consumes events, looks up the user's preferences (which channels they've enabled, quiet hours, do-not-disturb settings) from a preferences store, renders the message from a template, and dispatches to the appropriate channel-specific worker (push worker, email worker, SMS worker), each of which calls a third-party provider (APNs/FCM for push, an email API, an SMS gateway). Store a delivery log/status per notification for auditing and retry.

stages.prompt: How do you handle a burst of 10 million notifications needing to go out within a few minutes (e.g., a breaking news alert), without overwhelming downstream providers or losing messages?
stages.answer: Partition the queue (Kafka topics/partitions) so many consumer instances can process in parallel, and apply per-provider rate limiting/backpressure in each channel worker to stay within third-party API quotas (batch APIs where available, e.g., FCM's batch send). Use exponential backoff and dead-letter queues for failed sends so a provider hiccup doesn't drop messages, and prioritize/queue by urgency (time-sensitive alerts before digest-style notifications) so critical messages aren't stuck behind bulk traffic. Horizontally scale worker pools based on queue depth (autoscaling on backlog size, not just CPU).

stages.prompt: What managed cloud services would you use to build this?
stages.answer: AWS: SNS or Amazon Pinpoint for multi-channel delivery (push/email/SMS) with built-in provider integrations, SQS/Kinesis or MSK for the event queue, DynamoDB for preferences and delivery logs, Lambda for lightweight channel workers, and CloudWatch alarms on queue depth for autoscaling. Firebase Cloud Messaging (FCM) and Apple Push Notification service (APNs) remain the underlying push delivery mechanisms regardless of cloud provider.

diagram:
flowchart LR
    App[App Service] -->|event| Queue[(Kafka / SQS)]
    Queue --> NotifSvc[Notification Service]
    NotifSvc --> Prefs[(User Preferences)]
    NotifSvc --> PushW[Push Worker] --> FCM[FCM / APNs]
    NotifSvc --> EmailW[Email Worker] --> EmailAPI[Email Provider]
    NotifSvc --> SMSW[SMS Worker] --> SMSGW[SMS Gateway]
    NotifSvc --> Log[(Delivery Log)]

title: Design a Distributed Task Scheduler / Job Queue

stages.prompt: Design a distributed job scheduler that lets services enqueue tasks (e.g., 'send this email in 10 minutes', 'run this report at midnight') and guarantees each task runs at least once, at roughly the right time.
stages.answer: Core components: an API to enqueue a job (payload + run_at time + retry policy), a persistent job store (database table indexed by run_at/status), and a pool of worker processes that poll for due jobs (status=pending AND run_at <= now), claim them (e.g., via a conditional update setting status=in_progress with a lease/lock so no two workers grab the same job), execute them, and mark them complete or reschedule on failure. Use a heap/priority-queue-like index on run_at for efficient polling of 'what's due now'.

stages.prompt: How do you scale this to millions of scheduled jobs and ensure jobs aren't lost or double-executed if a worker crashes mid-task?
stages.answer: Shard the job store by job_id or run_at bucket across multiple database partitions so no single node holds all jobs. Use a lease-based claiming mechanism: a worker claims a job with a time-limited lease (e.g., 'in_progress until T+5min'); if the worker crashes, the lease expires and another worker can reclaim the job, giving at-least-once execution semantics (making job handlers idempotent is important since a job could theoretically run twice). For efficient polling at scale, avoid full-table scans by using a proper index (or a separate 'due soon' cache/priority queue like Redis sorted sets keyed by run_at) that's populated ahead of time rather than querying the whole job table every poll cycle.

stages.prompt: What existing tools or managed services would you use instead of building this from scratch?
stages.answer: Managed options: AWS EventBridge Scheduler or Step Functions for scheduled/orchestrated workflows, Google Cloud Tasks or Cloud Scheduler, or a well-established open-source job queue (Sidekiq, Celery with Redis/RabbitMQ, or Temporal for more complex durable workflows with retries and long-running state). Temporal in particular is popular for senior-level system design discussions because it explicitly solves the 'exactly-once-ish, crash-resilient, long-running workflow' problem with built-in state persistence.

diagram:
flowchart LR
    API[Enqueue API] --> Store[(Job Store\nsharded by job_id)]
    Store --> DueIdx[(Due-soon Index\nRedis sorted set)]
    Worker1[Worker Pool] -->|poll + lease claim| DueIdx
    Worker1 -->|execute| Handler[Job Handler]
    Handler -->|success| Store
    Handler -->|failure: retry/backoff| Store

title: Design a Payments / Ledger System

stages.prompt: Design the core of a payments system that records money moving between accounts (e.g., a wallet or marketplace ledger), ensuring balances are always accurate.
stages.answer: Use double-entry bookkeeping: every transaction creates two ledger entries (a debit and a credit) that must sum to zero, stored as immutable rows in an append-only ledger table (never update or delete existing entries - corrections are new offsetting entries). An account's balance is derived by summing its ledger entries (or maintained as a cached running balance updated transactionally alongside the ledger write). All writes happen inside a single database transaction so a transfer's debit and credit either both commit or neither does (atomicity), which is critical for financial correctness.

stages.prompt: How do you handle this at a scale of tens of thousands of transactions per second across many currencies and account shards, while keeping strong consistency for balance correctness?
stages.answer: Shard accounts across multiple database instances (e.g., by account_id hash), but a naive shard-per-account design breaks transactions that move money between accounts on different shards. Common approaches: use a distributed transaction protocol (two-phase commit) sparingly since it's slow, or model transfers as an asynchronous saga - reserve funds on the source shard, then apply the credit on the destination shard, with a compensating reversal if the second step fails, tracked via a transaction state machine. For read scaling, maintain eventually-consistent read replicas for balance lookups/statements while the ledger writes themselves stay strongly consistent on the primary shard owning that account.

stages.prompt: What existing infrastructure or protocols would a real fintech company lean on rather than building all of this from scratch?
stages.answer: Many companies use a dedicated ledger service (in-house or a vendor like Modern Treasury, or open-source ledger engines) that already encodes double-entry invariants, rather than reinventing balance logic per team. For actual money movement (as opposed to internal bookkeeping), rely on regulated payment rails and providers (Stripe, Adyen, ACH/SWIFT networks) rather than building settlement infrastructure yourself; your ledger records what happened, the payment processor handles moving real money and its own idempotency/retry guarantees (idempotency keys on payment API calls are essential to avoid double-charging on retries).

diagram:
flowchart TD
    Client -->|transfer request| API[Ledger API]
    API -->|idempotency key check| IdemStore[(Idempotency Store)]
    API -->|debit+credit, one txn| Ledger[(Append-only Ledger\nsharded by account)]
    Ledger --> BalanceCache[(Cached Balances)]
    API -->|cross-shard transfer| Saga[Saga Orchestrator]
    Saga -->|reserve| ShardA[(Account Shard A)]
    Saga -->|credit or compensate| ShardB[(Account Shard B)]

title: Design a Search Autocomplete / Typeahead System

stages.prompt: Design a search-box autocomplete system that, as a user types, suggests the top completions (like Google Search suggestions).
stages.answer: Precompute a trie (prefix tree) where each node represents a character and stores the top-k most frequent/popular completions for that prefix, built offline from historical query logs (aggregate query frequency, then propagate top completions up the trie). At request time, the client sends the current prefix, the server walks the trie to that node, and returns its precomputed top-k list - an O(prefix length) lookup, independent of the total number of possible completions.

stages.prompt: How do you keep suggestions fresh (reflecting trending/recent queries) without rebuilding the entire trie from scratch constantly, and how do you scale reads to handle every keystroke from millions of users?
stages.answer: Rebuild the trie incrementally/periodically (e.g., every few minutes) from a streaming aggregation of recent query counts (Kafka Streams/Flink maintaining a sliding-window frequency count), rather than a full batch rebuild each time, and merge in a decayed weighting so older queries gradually lose influence versus trending ones. Since it's read-extremely-heavy (every keystroke is a request) and read latency needs to be very low, keep the entire trie (or sharded portions of it, e.g., by first 1-2 characters) in memory across a fleet of read replicas, and put a CDN/edge cache in front of common short prefixes (like single letters) which are requested constantly and change rarely.

stages.prompt: What managed services or existing tools would you use?
stages.answer: Elasticsearch/OpenSearch has built-in completion suggester functionality that implements this trie-like prefix matching, and can offload most of the heavy lifting rather than hand-building a custom trie service. For the streaming aggregation layer, Kinesis Data Analytics or a managed Flink/Kafka Streams deployment computes trending-query frequencies; ElastiCache (Redis) can cache the hottest prefixes at the edge for sub-millisecond responses.

diagram:
flowchart LR
    Client -->|keystroke: prefix| Edge[CDN / Edge Cache]
    Edge -->|cache miss| API[Autocomplete API]
    API --> Trie[(In-memory Trie\nsharded by prefix)]
    LogStream[Query Log Stream] --> Aggregator[Streaming Aggregator]
    Aggregator -->|periodic rebuild| Trie

title: Design a Video Streaming Service

stages.prompt: Design the core architecture for a video streaming platform (like YouTube or Netflix): uploading a video and having viewers stream it smoothly on various devices/networks.
stages.answer: Upload flow: client uploads the raw video to object storage (S3-like), which triggers an async transcoding pipeline that converts it into multiple resolutions/bitrates (e.g., 240p-4K) and chunks it for adaptive bitrate streaming (HLS/DASH format), storing the resulting segments back in object storage alongside a manifest file listing available quality levels. Playback flow: the client's video player requests the manifest, then streams segments through a CDN, dynamically switching bitrate based on measured network conditions, so playback keeps up without buffering rather than always requesting the highest quality.

stages.prompt: How do you scale transcoding for millions of daily uploads (some very large) and ensure playback stays smooth for viewers worldwide with varying network quality?
stages.answer: Use a distributed transcoding worker pool (e.g., containers autoscaling on job queue depth) that processes chunks of a video in parallel rather than one worker handling an entire file serially, checkpointing progress so a crashed worker doesn't restart the whole job. Prioritize the encoding queue so short/popular content transcodes faster than a rarely-watched long video. For global playback smoothness, rely heavily on a multi-region CDN with edge caching (popular videos get cached close to viewers, reducing origin load and latency) and adaptive bitrate streaming client-side logic that continuously re-evaluates network throughput and switches quality mid-playback.

stages.prompt: What managed cloud services handle most of this instead of building it in-house?
stages.answer: AWS Elemental MediaConvert (transcoding), MediaPackage (packaging into HLS/DASH), and CloudFront (CDN delivery) form a common managed pipeline; S3 stores both raw uploads and processed segments. Equivalent stacks exist on GCP (Transcoder API + Cloud CDN) and Azure (Media Services). Very few companies build their own transcoding pipeline from scratch given how mature these managed offerings are - the differentiated engineering work is usually in recommendation, encoding-cost optimization, and player-side adaptive streaming logic rather than the pipeline itself.

diagram:
flowchart TD
    Uploader -->|raw video| RawStore[(Raw Video Storage - S3)]
    RawStore --> Queue[(Transcode Job Queue)]
    Queue --> Workers[Transcoding Workers\nparallel chunks]
    Workers --> SegStore[(Segment Storage\nmultiple bitrates)]
    SegStore --> CDN[Global CDN]
    Viewer -->|adaptive bitrate request| CDN
    Viewer -->|fetch manifest| API[Playback API]

title: Design a Distributed Logging / Monitoring Pipeline

stages.prompt: Design a system that collects logs and metrics from thousands of application servers, lets engineers search logs, and alerts on anomalies.
stages.answer: Each application server runs a lightweight log-shipping agent that tails log files (or receives structured log events directly) and forwards them to a durable ingestion buffer (Kafka), decoupling producers from the downstream processing rate. Consumers read from Kafka and index logs into a search-optimized store (Elasticsearch/OpenSearch) for querying, while a separate metrics pipeline aggregates numeric time-series data (request counts, latencies) into a time-series database (Prometheus-style) for dashboards. An alerting service evaluates rules against the metrics store (e.g., 'error rate > 5% for 5 minutes') and fires notifications.

stages.prompt: How do you handle a scale of terabytes of logs per day without the ingestion pipeline falling behind or costs spiraling out of control?
stages.answer: Apply sampling and log-level filtering at the source (don't ship every debug log at full fidelity in production; sample high-volume, low-value events) and use tiered storage - keep recent logs (e.g., last 7-14 days) in the hot, queryable search index, then roll older logs into cheap cold storage (S3/Glacier) that's only queried occasionally via batch jobs (Athena-style), rather than keeping everything in the expensive search cluster indefinitely. Scale the Kafka cluster and search cluster horizontally by partitioning by service/host, and pre-aggregate metrics at the edge (compute percentiles/counts locally before shipping) rather than shipping every raw data point, since most alerting only needs aggregates, not raw log lines.

stages.prompt: What managed/off-the-shelf tools would you use instead of building this pipeline yourself?
stages.answer: The 'ELK stack' (Elasticsearch, Logstash/Fluentd for shipping, Kibana for visualization) or its managed equivalent (Elastic Cloud, AWS OpenSearch Service) is the standard building block for log search. For metrics and alerting, Prometheus + Grafana (or managed equivalents like Amazon Managed Service for Prometheus, Datadog, or New Relic) handle time-series storage, dashboards, and alerting rules out of the box, so most teams integrate these rather than building a custom pipeline end-to-end.

diagram:
flowchart LR
    App1[App Server] -->|agent| Kafka[(Kafka Ingestion Buffer)]
    App2[App Server] -->|agent| Kafka
    Kafka --> LogIndex[(Search Index\nElasticsearch)]
    Kafka --> MetricsDB[(Time-series DB\nPrometheus)]
    LogIndex --> Dashboard[Search UI - Kibana]
    MetricsDB --> Grafana[Dashboards - Grafana]
    MetricsDB --> Alerting[Alerting Rules Engine]
    Alerting -->|fires| Notify[On-call Notification]

title: Design a Collaborative Document Editor

stages.prompt: Design a collaborative document editor (like Google Docs) where multiple users can edit the same document simultaneously and see each other's changes in near real-time.
stages.answer: Each client holds a local copy of the document and sends edit operations (not the whole document) to a central server over a WebSocket connection - e.g., 'insert character X at position 5'. The server broadcasts each operation to all other connected clients editing that document, and every client applies incoming operations to its local copy in the order received, keeping copies in sync. The server also persists a snapshot plus an operation log periodically so the document can be reconstructed and late-joining clients can catch up.

stages.prompt: Two users edit the same region of the document at nearly the same time - how do you resolve the conflict so both edits are preserved correctly and everyone converges to the same final document?
stages.answer: This is the core hard problem, typically solved with Operational Transformation (OT) or Conflict-free Replicated Data Types (CRDTs). OT transforms an incoming operation against any operations that happened concurrently before applying it, so that applying operations in different orders on different clients still converges to the same result (this is what Google Docs originally used). CRDTs instead design the data structure itself (e.g., a sequence CRDT for text) so that concurrent operations are commutative and always merge deterministically without needing a central transform step, which simplifies peer-to-peer and offline scenarios at the cost of some memory overhead for tombstones/metadata. Most new collaborative editors (e.g., Figma's multiplayer engine) favor CRDT-based approaches for this reason.

stages.prompt: What existing libraries or services would you use rather than implementing OT/CRDT logic from scratch?
stages.answer: Well-tested open-source CRDT libraries like Yjs or Automerge implement the hard conflict-resolution logic and are widely used in production collaborative apps, so most teams integrate one of these rather than writing OT/CRDT algorithms themselves (which are notoriously easy to get subtly wrong). For the real-time transport layer, managed WebSocket/pub-sub services (AWS AppSync, Pusher, Ably, or Firebase Realtime Database) handle connection fan-out and presence (who's currently viewing) without building a custom WebSocket gateway.

diagram:
flowchart LR
    ClientA[Client A] <-->|WebSocket: ops| Server[Collab Server]
    ClientB[Client B] <-->|WebSocket: ops| Server
    Server -->|CRDT merge| DocState[(In-memory Doc State)]
    Server -->|periodic snapshot| Persist[(Document Store)]
    ClientA -.->|local CRDT replica| ClientA
    ClientB -.->|local CRDT replica| ClientB

title: Design a Content Delivery Network (CDN)

stages.prompt: Explain how a CDN works and design the core routing/caching logic that lets it serve static content (images, videos, JS bundles) with low latency worldwide.
stages.answer: A CDN operates a large number of geographically distributed edge servers (Points of Presence). When a client requests a resource, DNS-based or anycast routing directs them to the nearest/best-performing edge server rather than the origin server. That edge server checks its local cache: on a hit, it serves the content directly (fast, low latency, no origin load); on a miss, it fetches from the origin server (or a regional mid-tier cache), stores a copy locally per the cache policy (TTL-based), and serves it - subsequent nearby requests then hit the cache.

stages.prompt: How does the CDN decide what to cache and for how long, and how do you handle invalidating cached content when the origin updates (e.g., a new app version needs old cached JS purged)?
stages.answer: Caching behavior is controlled via HTTP cache headers set by the origin (Cache-Control, ETag, max-age) - the CDN respects these to decide TTL and whether to revalidate. For content that rarely changes, use long TTLs plus cache-busting via versioned URLs (e.g., app.v123.js) so a new deploy is simply a new URL, avoiding invalidation entirely for immutable assets. For content that must be invalidated on demand (breaking news correction, a deleted image), CDNs provide an explicit purge/invalidation API that propagates a 'remove this from cache' instruction to all edge nodes, though this is typically slower and more expensive than TTL-based expiry, so it's used sparingly rather than as the primary strategy.

stages.prompt: What managed CDN services would you use in practice?
stages.answer: CloudFront (AWS), Cloud CDN (GCP), Azure CDN, or third-party specialists like Cloudflare and Fastly - all provide the edge network, routing, and cache-invalidation APIs out of the box. Building your own global edge network is almost never justified given how commoditized and mature these offerings are; the engineering work in practice is mostly around correct cache-header configuration, cache-key design (e.g., varying cache by query params or headers when needed), and choosing a purge strategy.

diagram:
flowchart LR
    Client -->|DNS/anycast routing| Edge[Nearest Edge Server]
    Edge -->|cache hit| Client
    Edge -->|cache miss| Origin[(Origin Server)]
    Origin --> Edge
    Deploy[New Deploy] -->|purge API| Edge
    Origin -->|cache headers: TTL, ETag| Edge

title: Design an E-commerce Inventory and Order System

stages.prompt: Design the core of an e-commerce backend: product inventory tracking and order placement, ensuring you never oversell an item that's out of stock.
stages.answer: Store inventory as a row per SKU with an available_quantity column. Placing an order requires atomically decrementing inventory only if sufficient stock exists - typically a conditional update ('UPDATE inventory SET quantity = quantity - N WHERE sku = ? AND quantity >= N') inside the same database transaction as creating the order record, so either both succeed or neither does. If the conditional update affects zero rows, the order is rejected as out-of-stock before any payment is attempted.

stages.prompt: During a flash sale, thousands of users try to buy the last 50 units of a popular item simultaneously. How do you keep this correct and fast without the inventory row becoming a massive contention bottleneck?
stages.answer: A single database row being updated by thousands of concurrent transactions will serialize and become a bottleneck (lock contention). Common mitigations: reserve stock with a short-lived hold (e.g., 'reserved for 5 minutes while payment completes') using an atomic decrement in a fast in-memory store (Redis, via DECR or a Lua script for atomicity) rather than hitting the primary database directly for every attempt, then asynchronously reconcile with the database. Alternatively, shard hot inventory counters (split the 50 units conceptually across multiple counter partitions) to spread contention, recombining if a shard runs out. Queue excess demand (a virtual waiting room) so the backend only processes as many concurrent purchase attempts as it can safely serialize.

stages.prompt: What existing infrastructure patterns or services help with this?
stages.answer: Redis's atomic operations (INCR/DECR, or Lua scripts for compound check-and-decrement) are a standard building block for high-contention counters like flash-sale inventory. For the order/payment flow itself, most companies integrate a payment processor (Stripe, Adyen) that handles the actual charge with idempotency keys, while your own service focuses on the reservation/inventory logic; message queues (SQS/Kafka) decouple 'order placed' from downstream fulfillment/shipping systems so inventory checks stay fast and fulfillment processing happens asynchronously.

diagram:
flowchart TD
    Client -->|purchase request| API[Order API]
    API -->|atomic reserve| Redis[(Redis Inventory Counter)]
    Redis -->|reserved| Payment[Payment Processor]
    Payment -->|success| OrderDB[(Order DB)]
    Payment -->|success| Reconcile[Async Reconciler]
    Reconcile --> InventoryDB[(Inventory DB - source of truth)]
    Payment -->|failure/timeout| Release[Release Reservation]
    Release --> Redis

title: Design a Distributed Lock Service

stages.prompt: Design a distributed lock service that lets multiple processes across different machines coordinate exclusive access to a shared resource (e.g., only one worker should process a given task at a time).
stages.answer: A lock service exposes acquire(resource_id, ttl) and release(resource_id, token) operations. Acquiring a lock means atomically checking that no valid lock currently exists for that resource_id and, if so, writing a lock entry with a unique token and an expiry (TTL) - implemented as a single atomic operation (e.g., Redis's SET key value NX EX ttl, or a conditional write in a strongly consistent store). The TTL is critical: if the process holding the lock crashes, the lock automatically expires rather than being held forever, letting another process acquire it.

stages.prompt: What happens if the process holding the lock is just slow (e.g., a long GC pause) and the lock expires while it's still working, letting a second process acquire the same lock and both now think they have exclusive access?
stages.answer: This is the classic 'lock validity vs. liveness' problem. Mitigations: use a fencing token - each successful lock acquisition returns a monotonically increasing number, and the protected resource (e.g., the storage system being written to) rejects any write that arrives with an older fencing token than one it's already seen, so even if two clients briefly believe they hold the lock, only the legitimate latest holder's writes are accepted. Additionally, the lock holder can periodically 'renew' (extend) the lease while still working, reducing (but not eliminating) the chance of premature expiry; the fencing-token check at the resource is what actually guarantees correctness, since clock/pause-based assumptions alone (like Redis's single-instance Redlock algorithm) are debated as insufficient for strict correctness guarantees.

stages.prompt: What existing tools would you use instead of building this yourself?
stages.answer: ZooKeeper and etcd are purpose-built for distributed coordination (leader election, distributed locks, configuration) and provide fencing-token-like sequence numbers natively (ZooKeeper's sequential ephemeral nodes) - most production systems (Kafka historically, Kubernetes) rely on one of these rather than a hand-rolled lock service. For simpler, lower-stakes use cases, a managed Redis with the Redlock algorithm (or a single Redis instance's SET NX EX) is a common lighter-weight choice, with the caveat that it offers weaker guarantees than ZooKeeper/etcd for scenarios where correctness under network partitions is critical.

diagram:
flowchart LR
    ClientA[Process A] -->|acquire resource_id| LockSvc[Lock Service - etcd/ZooKeeper]
    ClientB[Process B] -->|acquire resource_id| LockSvc
    LockSvc -->|grants w/ fencing token=42| ClientA
    ClientA -->|write w/ token=42| Resource[(Protected Resource)]
    ClientB -->|write w/ stale token=41| Resource
    Resource -->|rejects stale token| ClientB

title: Design a Metrics Aggregation System (Like Counter at Scale)

stages.prompt: Design a system to track and display 'like' counts on social media posts, where popular posts can receive thousands of likes per second.
stages.answer: Naively incrementing a single database row per like (UPDATE posts SET likes = likes + 1) becomes a severe write bottleneck for a viral post under heavy concurrent load. Instead, buffer like events into a fast in-memory counter (Redis INCR per post_id) which handles very high write throughput, and periodically (e.g., every few seconds) flush the accumulated count to the durable database asynchronously, rather than hitting the database on every single like.

stages.prompt: How do you scale this to millions of distinct posts being liked simultaneously, and how do you keep the displayed count reasonably accurate without re-querying Redis on every single page view?
stages.answer: Shard the Redis layer by post_id (consistent hashing) so no single Redis instance becomes a hotspot even under many simultaneously-viral posts. For serving the count to viewers, don't query Redis on every page render - instead maintain a short-lived cached/denormalized count (e.g., in the CDN or an application-level cache with a few seconds of staleness) since like counts don't need to be real-time-exact for display purposes (a count that's a few seconds stale is an acceptable tradeoff for massively reduced read load). Use an event-driven pipeline (Kafka) to also feed the actual like events into analytics/ranking systems asynchronously, decoupled from the fast counter path.

stages.prompt: What existing patterns or managed services fit this?
stages.answer: This is a textbook use case for Redis (or a managed equivalent like ElastiCache/Memorystore) as a high-throughput counter cache in front of a durable database, combined with a periodic write-back/flush job. For the broader class of 'aggregate a high-volume event stream into rollup counts,' stream-processing frameworks (Kafka Streams, Flink) with windowed aggregations are the standard tool, letting you compute not just raw counts but time-windowed trending metrics from the same event stream.

diagram:
flowchart LR
    Client -->|like event| API[Like API]
    API -->|INCR post_id| Redis[(Sharded Redis Counters)]
    Redis -->|periodic flush| DB[(Durable Post DB)]
    API -->|event| Kafka[(Kafka - like events)]
    Kafka --> Analytics[Analytics / Ranking Pipeline]
    Viewer -->|read count| Cache[Short-TTL Read Cache]
    Cache --> Redis

title: Design a Web Analytics / Event Tracking Pipeline

stages.prompt: Design a system that ingests client-side analytics events (page views, button clicks) from a website with millions of daily visitors and makes them queryable for a dashboard (e.g., 'daily active users', 'conversion funnel').
stages.answer: The client-side SDK batches events locally and periodically sends them (e.g., every few seconds or on a batch-size threshold) to a lightweight ingestion endpoint, rather than firing a network request per single click, to reduce request volume. The ingestion service validates and writes events to a durable, high-throughput event log (Kafka), decoupling ingestion from downstream processing. A stream-processing job consumes from Kafka and writes events into an analytics-optimized data store (a columnar warehouse like BigQuery/Redshift/ClickHouse) that dashboards query.

stages.prompt: How do you handle billions of events per day while keeping dashboard queries (like 'daily active users over the last 30 days') fast, rather than scanning raw event data every time?
stages.answer: Precompute rollups/aggregates on a schedule or continuously via stream processing (e.g., hourly/daily unique-user counts, funnel step counts) and store those aggregates separately from raw events, so dashboards query small precomputed tables instead of scanning billions of raw rows on every load. Use approximate algorithms for expensive distinct-count queries (HyperLogLog for approximate unique visitor counts) which trade a small, bounded error for dramatically less memory/compute than exact distinct counting at this scale. Partition the raw event warehouse table by date so queries scanning a specific time range don't touch unrelated partitions.

stages.prompt: What managed/off-the-shelf tools would you use rather than building this pipeline yourself?
stages.answer: Managed analytics platforms (Google Analytics, Amplitude, Mixpanel, Segment as a collection/routing layer) already solve most of this end-to-end for many companies, and are a legitimate answer if the interviewer is asking about product decisions rather than infrastructure. For a custom in-house pipeline: Kafka or Kinesis for ingestion, a managed stream processor (Kinesis Data Analytics, Dataflow, or Flink), and a cloud data warehouse (BigQuery, Redshift, Snowflake) or a purpose-built real-time analytics database (ClickHouse, Druid) for the serving layer, with a BI tool (Looker, Tableau, Metabase) on top for dashboards.

diagram:
flowchart LR
    Browser -->|batched events| Ingest[Ingestion API]
    Ingest --> Kafka[(Kafka Event Log)]
    Kafka --> Stream[Stream Processor - Flink]
    Stream --> RawWarehouse[(Raw Event Warehouse\npartitioned by date)]
    Stream --> Rollups[(Precomputed Rollups\nHyperLogLog counts)]
    Dashboard[Analytics Dashboard] --> Rollups
    Dashboard -.->|ad-hoc deep query| RawWarehouse

title: Design a Distributed ID Generator

stages.prompt: Design a service that generates globally unique IDs across many servers, at high throughput, without a central bottleneck.
stages.answer: A common approach (Twitter's Snowflake) encodes an ID as a single 64-bit integer composed of a timestamp (milliseconds since a custom epoch), a machine/worker ID, and a per-millisecond sequence counter, all packed into fixed bit ranges. Each server generates its own IDs locally by combining the current timestamp with its assigned worker ID and an incrementing sequence number, requiring no coordination or round-trip to a central service, while the timestamp component keeps IDs roughly time-sortable.

stages.prompt: How do you guarantee no two servers ever produce the same worker ID (which would cause ID collisions), especially as servers are added/removed dynamically in a cloud environment?
stages.answer: Use a coordination service (ZooKeeper or etcd) to atomically assign each server a unique worker ID from a bounded pool when it starts up, releasing the ID when the server shuts down cleanly; if a server crashes without releasing, a lease/TTL on the assignment lets it expire and be reclaimed. Alternatively, in containerized/cloud environments, derive the worker ID deterministically from a stable identifier the orchestrator already guarantees is unique (e.g., a Kubernetes StatefulSet's pod ordinal), avoiding the need for a coordination service at all.

stages.prompt: What existing tools or managed services would you use instead of implementing Snowflake-style generation yourself?
stages.answer: Many databases have this built in: PostgreSQL/MySQL auto-increment or sequence objects work for single-database setups; for distributed systems, MongoDB's ObjectId, Cassandra/ScyllaDB's TimeUUID, or a managed distributed SQL database (CockroachDB, Google Spanner) that generates globally unique, roughly-ordered IDs natively are common choices, letting you avoid operating a separate ID-generation service entirely.

diagram:
flowchart LR
    Client -->|request ID| Server1[App Server 1\nworker_id=1]
    Client -->|request ID| Server2[App Server 2\nworker_id=2]
    Server1 -->|local: timestamp+workerID+seq| ID1[64-bit ID]
    Server2 -->|local: timestamp+workerID+seq| ID2[64-bit ID]
    Coord[Coordination Service\netcd/ZooKeeper] -.->|assigns worker_id on startup| Server1
    Coord -.->|assigns worker_id on startup| Server2

title: Design a Distributed File Storage System (like Dropbox)

stages.prompt: Design a file-sync service (like Dropbox) that lets users upload files, sync them across multiple devices, and share them with others.
stages.answer: Split files into fixed-size chunks and store each chunk in a content-addressed object store (keyed by hash of contents), with a separate metadata service tracking {file_id, owner, chunk_hashes in order, version, permissions}. Chunking with content-addressing means only changed chunks need re-upload/re-download when a file is edited (deduplication), and identical chunks across different users' files are stored only once. Each client keeps a local sync agent that watches the filesystem for changes, computes changed chunks, and syncs deltas with the server rather than re-uploading whole files.

stages.prompt: How do you handle two devices editing the same file offline and then both coming back online with conflicting changes?
stages.answer: Detect conflicts via version vectors or a simple last-write-wins with version numbers: each sync records the version it was based on, and if a client tries to push a change based on a stale version (because someone else's edit already landed), the server rejects it and the client resolves by creating a conflicted copy (e.g., 'file (conflicted copy).txt') rather than silently overwriting data, letting the user manually reconcile. For structured documents, more sophisticated CRDT/OT-based merging (as in a collaborative editor) can auto-merge non-conflicting changes, but for opaque binary files, conflicted-copy is the standard pragmatic approach.

stages.prompt: What managed services would you use to implement the chunk storage and sync infrastructure?
stages.answer: S3 (or GCS/Azure Blob) for content-addressed chunk storage with built-in deduplication-friendly addressing, DynamoDB or a similar low-latency store for the file/chunk metadata index, and a WebSocket or long-polling notification service (API Gateway WebSockets, or a managed pub/sub like SNS) to push 'something changed, sync now' events to other online devices in near real time, rather than having every device poll constantly.

diagram:
flowchart TD
    ClientA[Device A] -->|changed chunks| SyncAPI[Sync API]
    ClientB[Device B] -->|changed chunks| SyncAPI
    SyncAPI --> ChunkStore[(Content-addressed Chunk Store - S3)]
    SyncAPI --> MetaDB[(File Metadata DB\nversions, chunk lists)]
    SyncAPI -->|change notification| PubSub[(Pub/Sub)]
    PubSub -->|sync now| ClientB
    ClientA -.->|conflict detected| ConflictCopy[Conflicted Copy Created]

title: Design a Distributed Configuration Management Service

stages.prompt: Design a centralized configuration service that lets hundreds of microservices fetch and be notified of configuration changes (feature flags, rate limits, connection strings) without redeploying.
stages.answer: A config service stores key-value configuration (often namespaced per-service/per-environment) in a strongly consistent store, exposes a read API for services to fetch current config on startup, and supports a watch/subscribe mechanism (long-polling or streaming) so services are notified quickly when a value changes rather than needing to poll constantly. Each service caches the last-known config locally so it can keep running with stale-but-valid config even if the config service is briefly unreachable.

stages.prompt: How do you roll out a risky configuration change (like a new rate limit) safely to thousands of service instances without causing a global outage if the value turns out to be wrong?
stages.answer: Support staged/percentage-based rollout: apply the new config value to a small percentage of instances or a specific canary service group first, monitor error rates/metrics, and progressively widen the rollout - functionally the same canary-deployment principle used for code deploys, applied to config. Critically, keep a fast, well-tested rollback path (revert to the previous config version) that can be triggered in seconds, since a bad config change should be as cheap to undo as it was to apply.

stages.prompt: What existing tools would you use instead of building a config service from scratch?
stages.answer: etcd or Consul are purpose-built for exactly this (strongly consistent key-value storage with a watch API, used heavily by Kubernetes itself for cluster config). Feature-flag-specific platforms (LaunchDarkly, Unleash, or AWS AppConfig) add staged-rollout and targeting rules (e.g., 'enable for 5% of users in this region') on top of basic config storage, which is usually preferable to re-implementing percentage rollout logic yourself.

diagram:
flowchart LR
    Config[Config Store - etcd/Consul] -->|watch/stream| Svc1[Service Instance 1]
    Config -->|watch/stream| Svc2[Service Instance 2]
    Config -->|watch/stream| Svc3[Service Instance 3 - canary]
    Operator[Operator] -->|staged rollout: canary first| Config
    Svc1 -.->|cached fallback config| Svc1
    Metrics[Metrics/Alerting] -.->|bad rollout detected| Operator

title: Design a Multiplayer Game Backend (Real-Time State Sync)

stages.prompt: Design the backend for a real-time multiplayer game (e.g., a battle-royale style game) where many players' actions must be synchronized with low latency.
stages.answer: Each active match runs on a dedicated game-server process (often one process per match instance, since game state needs low-latency, tightly-coupled simulation) that all players in that match connect to via UDP or a low-latency protocol, sending input commands and receiving authoritative world-state updates at a fixed tick rate. The server is authoritative over game state (to prevent cheating) - clients render a locally-predicted view for responsiveness (client-side prediction) and reconcile/correct when the server's authoritative update arrives, rather than waiting for a round-trip before showing any effect of a player's action.

stages.prompt: How do you matchmake players into game-server instances at scale, and handle a game server crashing mid-match?
stages.answer: A separate matchmaking service queues players (often grouped by skill rating/region) and, once a suitable group is formed, requests a game-server instance from a fleet manager (which allocates a server process/container, possibly spinning up new capacity if the fleet is near saturation) and hands connection details back to the matched players. For crash resilience, the fleet manager monitors server health; if a game server crashes mid-match, there's typically no seamless mid-match recovery for real-time games (state is often lost), but the fleet manager detects the failure quickly and can notify affected players so they aren't left hanging indefinitely, and logs enough match state periodically for post-match stats/anti-cheat review even if the live match can't be resumed.

stages.prompt: What managed game-backend services would you use rather than building the fleet/matchmaking infrastructure yourself?
stages.answer: Amazon GameLift (or GameLift FleetIQ) and similar managed game-server-hosting platforms handle fleet scaling, health monitoring, and matchmaking queue integration specifically for this use case, so most studios integrate one of these rather than building custom fleet management. For the low-latency networking layer itself, engines commonly use UDP-based libraries (ENet, or engine-native networking in Unreal/Unity) rather than building a custom transport protocol from scratch.

diagram:
flowchart LR
    PlayerA[Player A] -->|queue| Matchmaker[Matchmaking Service]
    PlayerB[Player B] -->|queue| Matchmaker
    Matchmaker -->|allocate| Fleet[Fleet Manager]
    Fleet -->|spin up| GameServer[Dedicated Game Server\nauthoritative state]
    PlayerA <-->|UDP: inputs / state updates| GameServer
    PlayerB <-->|UDP: inputs / state updates| GameServer
    Fleet -.->|health check| GameServer

title: Design a Recommendation System

stages.prompt: Design a product/content recommendation system (like 'you might also like') for an e-commerce or streaming platform.
stages.answer: Two classic approaches, often combined: collaborative filtering (recommend items liked by users with similar behavior to the target user, using a user-item interaction matrix) and content-based filtering (recommend items similar in attributes/features to what the user already liked). At serving time, a candidate-generation step retrieves a broad set of plausible items quickly (e.g., via an approximate-nearest-neighbor search over learned embeddings, or precomputed 'users who liked X also liked Y' lookups), then a ranking model scores and orders that smaller candidate set using richer features, since running a heavy model over the entire catalog per request would be too slow.

stages.prompt: How do you keep recommendations fresh (reflecting a user's behavior from the last few minutes, like items they just viewed) while still being backed by embeddings/models that are expensive to retrain frequently?
stages.answer: Separate the system into a slow-changing offline layer (batch-trained embeddings/models, retrained daily or hourly on aggregate historical data) and a fast online layer (a real-time feature store capturing the user's most recent actions - clicks, views - within the current session) that's blended into the ranking step at request time. This way, the expensive model training happens on a relaxed schedule, while recent-behavior signals (which don't need a full retrain to be useful, e.g., simple re-ranking boosts for recently-viewed categories) keep results feeling responsive to what the user is doing right now.

stages.prompt: What existing infrastructure/services would you use rather than building the whole recommendation pipeline from scratch?
stages.answer: Managed ML platforms (AWS Personalize, Google Recommendations AI/Vertex AI Matching Engine) provide built-in collaborative-filtering and candidate-generation pipelines specifically for recommendations, letting teams skip building the training infrastructure. For custom pipelines, a vector database (Pinecone, Weaviate, or pgvector on Postgres) handles the approximate-nearest-neighbor candidate generation over learned embeddings, while a feature store (Feast, or a cloud-native equivalent) supplies the real-time behavioral features to the ranking step, and the ranking model itself is typically served via a standard model-serving platform (SageMaker, Vertex AI Prediction, or a lightweight in-house service).

diagram:
flowchart TD
    Batch[Offline Batch Training] -->|embeddings| VectorDB[(Vector DB - candidate gen)]
    Client -->|request recs| CandidateSvc[Candidate Generation]
    CandidateSvc --> VectorDB
    RecentActivity[(Real-time Feature Store\nrecent clicks/views)] --> RankSvc[Ranking Service]
    CandidateSvc -->|candidates| RankSvc
    RankSvc -->|ranked list| Client

title: Design an Ad Click Tracking and Billing System

stages.prompt: Design a system that tracks ad impressions and clicks at scale and accurately bills advertisers based on a cost-per-click model.
stages.answer: When an ad is served, log an impression event (ad_id, user_id, timestamp, a unique impression_id) to a durable event pipeline. When a click occurs, the click event references the impression_id, letting the system attribute the click to the exact ad serving that generated it and reject fraudulent or duplicate clicks not tied to a valid recent impression. Billing aggregates valid clicks per advertiser per billing period and deducts from their budget, with the aggregation computed asynchronously from the raw event stream rather than synchronously on every click, since billing correctness matters more than billing latency.

stages.prompt: How do you prevent click fraud (bots or malicious actors generating fake clicks to drain a competitor's budget) and ensure an advertiser is never billed twice for the same click, even with retries in a distributed system?
stages.answer: For fraud, apply multiple signals: rate-limit clicks per user/IP within short windows, use bot-detection heuristics (mouse movement, timing patterns, known bot IP ranges), and require a valid, recent, non-reused impression_id per click, flagging statistical anomalies (e.g., a single IP generating an implausible click-through rate) for review or automatic exclusion from billing. For exactly-once billing despite retries, generate an idempotency key per click event (e.g., derived from impression_id) and use it to deduplicate at the aggregation stage, so a network retry that resends the same click event doesn't get double-counted, similar to idempotency-key patterns used in payment processing.

stages.prompt: What data infrastructure would you use to handle this at scale (billions of ad events per day)?
stages.answer: Kafka (or Kinesis) ingests the raw impression/click event stream at high throughput; a stream processor (Flink or Kafka Streams) performs real-time deduplication and fraud-signal scoring; aggregated, validated click counts land in a data warehouse (BigQuery/Redshift/Snowflake) for billing computation and advertiser-facing reporting dashboards. Real-time budget enforcement (pausing an ad campaign once its budget is exhausted) typically uses a fast in-memory counter (Redis) updated as valid clicks are confirmed, similar to the rate-limiter and hot-counter patterns used elsewhere in high-throughput systems.

diagram:
flowchart LR
    AdServe[Ad Served] -->|impression event| Kafka[(Kafka)]
    ClickEvent[Click Event] -->|references impression_id| Kafka
    Kafka --> Stream[Stream Processor\ndedup + fraud scoring]
    Stream --> Warehouse[(Data Warehouse\nbilling aggregation)]
    Stream --> BudgetCounter[(Redis Budget Counter)]
    BudgetCounter -->|budget exhausted| PauseCampaign[Pause Ad Campaign]

title: Design a Health-Check / Service Discovery System

stages.prompt: Design a service discovery system that lets microservices find healthy instances of other services to call, in an environment where instances are constantly starting, stopping, and scaling.
stages.answer: Each service instance registers itself with a service registry on startup (announcing its address and the service name it provides) and periodically sends heartbeats to prove it's still alive; the registry removes entries whose heartbeat has expired. When service A wants to call service B, it queries the registry for currently healthy instances of B and picks one (often via client-side load balancing - round robin or least-connections among the returned healthy instances) rather than going through a single central load balancer for every call.

stages.prompt: What happens if the service registry itself becomes unavailable or slow - does every service call in your whole system now fail?
stages.answer: This is why clients should cache the last-known-healthy instance list locally and continue using it (with periodic background refresh) rather than querying the registry synchronously on every single call - a brief registry outage then just means slightly stale routing information rather than a total system outage. The registry itself should also be run as a highly-available cluster (e.g., a Raft-based consensus cluster like etcd/Consul, which tolerates the failure of a minority of its nodes) rather than a single instance, since it's a piece of shared infrastructure that many services depend on.

stages.prompt: What existing tools handle this instead of building a custom registry?
stages.answer: Consul, etcd, and Apache ZooKeeper are the standard building blocks for service registration and discovery, each providing consistent storage plus health-checking and watch APIs. In Kubernetes environments, this is largely built in already: Kubernetes Services and DNS-based discovery, combined with the kubelet's liveness/readiness probes, provide registration and health-checking without needing a separate registry component at all.

diagram:
flowchart LR
    SvcB1[Service B Instance 1] -->|register + heartbeat| Registry[(Service Registry - Consul/etcd)]
    SvcB2[Service B Instance 2] -->|register + heartbeat| Registry
    SvcA[Service A] -->|query: healthy B instances| Registry
    Registry -->|B1, B2| SvcA
    SvcA -->|client-side load balance| SvcB1
    SvcA -.->|cached fallback list| SvcA

title: Design a Distributed Web Cache Invalidation System

stages.prompt: Design a system to keep cached data (e.g., in a CDN or application cache layer) consistent when the underlying source data changes across a large, geographically distributed cache fleet.
stages.answer: Most systems favor TTL-based expiry as the default (cached entries simply expire after a set time and are refetched), which requires no coordination at all and tolerates some staleness by design. For cases needing faster invalidation, tag cached entries with the resource(s) they depend on, and when that resource changes, publish an invalidation event (resource key) to a pub/sub system that all cache nodes subscribe to, so each node can purge matching entries from its local cache upon receiving the event.

stages.prompt: At a scale of thousands of cache nodes across many regions, how do you avoid the invalidation event itself becoming a bottleneck or arriving too slowly to matter?
stages.answer: Use a fan-out pub/sub backbone (Kafka, or a purpose-built CDN invalidation API) that's designed for high-throughput broadcast rather than point-to-point delivery to each node, and batch invalidations where possible (e.g., invalidate by a prefix or a versioned cache key rather than issuing one event per exact key) to reduce event volume. A common practical trick that sidesteps propagation-speed concerns almost entirely is cache-key versioning: instead of invalidating an old cache entry, encode a version number in the cache key itself (e.g., derived from a 'last updated' timestamp on the source data); a data change simply produces a new key that nobody has cached yet, and the old entry harmlessly expires via its normal TTL rather than needing to be actively purged anywhere.

stages.prompt: What existing services/patterns would you rely on rather than building fleet-wide invalidation from scratch?
stages.answer: CDNs (CloudFront, Fastly, Cloudflare) provide built-in purge/invalidation APIs that handle fleet-wide propagation for you; application-level caches typically use Redis pub/sub or a message queue (Kafka/SNS) to broadcast invalidation events to subscriber instances. In practice, most senior engineers reach for TTL-plus-versioned-keys as the default strategy specifically because it avoids needing a fleet-wide invalidation system at all for the majority of use cases.

diagram:
flowchart LR
    Source[Source Data Changes] -->|publish invalidation| PubSub[(Pub/Sub - Kafka/Redis)]
    PubSub --> Cache1[Cache Node - Region A]
    PubSub --> Cache2[Cache Node - Region B]
    PubSub --> Cache3[Cache Node - Region C]
    Source -.->|alt: versioned cache key| VersionedKey[New key: resource:v124]
    VersionedKey -.->|old key expires via TTL| OldEntry[resource:v123 - stale, unused]

title: Design a Two-Factor Authentication (2FA) System

stages.prompt: Design a two-factor authentication system supporting time-based one-time passwords (TOTP, like Google Authenticator) as a second login factor.
stages.answer: On enrollment, generate a random secret key for the user, share it with their authenticator app (via QR code), and store it securely (encrypted at rest) associated with the user's account. TOTP codes are generated by both the app and the server independently using the shared secret combined with the current time (rounded to a time step, typically 30 seconds), via an HMAC-based algorithm - since both sides compute the same code from the same secret and time without any network communication needed, verification is just comparing the server's independently-computed expected code against what the user submits.

stages.prompt: How do you handle clock drift between the user's device and your server (which would cause valid codes to be rejected), and how do you prevent a leaked/intercepted code from being replayed?
stages.answer: Accept codes from a small window of adjacent time steps (e.g., the current step plus one step before/after) to tolerate minor clock drift, rather than requiring exact synchronization - this is a standard, bounded tolerance built into the TOTP spec's usage recommendations. To prevent replay, once a code has been successfully used, record it (or its time step) as consumed for that user and reject any repeat submission of the same code within its validity window, so even if an attacker intercepts a valid code, it can't be reused after the legitimate login completes.

stages.prompt: What existing libraries or services would you use rather than implementing the TOTP algorithm and secret management yourself?
stages.answer: TOTP (RFC 6238) is a well-standardized algorithm with mature, audited libraries in every major language (e.g., pyotp for Python, otplib for JavaScript) - implementing the cryptographic HMAC logic yourself is unnecessary and risky. For enrollment/secret storage and broader 2FA flows (including SMS or push-based factors as alternatives), managed identity platforms (Auth0, AWS Cognito, Okta) provide this out of the box, which is often preferable to self-hosting secret storage given the security sensitivity of the data involved.

diagram:
flowchart LR
    User[User Enrolls] -->|shared secret via QR code| App[Authenticator App]
    User -->|shared secret| Server[(Encrypted Secret Store)]
    App -->|HMAC secret+time| Code1[Generated Code]
    Server -->|HMAC secret+time| Code2[Expected Code]
    User -->|submits code| LoginAPI[Login API]
    LoginAPI -->|compare| Code2
    LoginAPI -->|check not reused| UsedCodes[(Consumed Codes Log)]

title: Design a Distributed Job Deduplication System

stages.prompt: Design a system to prevent the same logical job (e.g., 'send welcome email to user X') from being processed twice, even if it's accidentally enqueued multiple times by an upstream producer.
stages.answer: Assign each job a deterministic idempotency key derived from its logical identity (e.g., a hash of user_id + email_type + a coarse time bucket, so retries of 'the same' job produce the same key) rather than a random ID. Before processing a job, atomically check-and-set that key in a fast store (e.g., Redis SETNX with a TTL, or a unique-constraint insert into a database table) - if the key already exists, skip processing since it's a duplicate; if not, the atomic set both claims the job and records that it's being handled.

stages.prompt: What happens if a worker claims a job (successfully sets the dedup key) but then crashes before finishing it — does the job get silently lost forever since the key now permanently blocks reprocessing?
stages.answer: Set the dedup key with a TTL slightly longer than the expected job processing time, and only mark it as 'permanently complete' (or extend/remove the TTL appropriately) after the job actually finishes successfully. If a worker crashes mid-processing, the dedup key simply expires after its TTL, allowing another worker to pick up and reprocess the job - trading a small risk of duplicate processing after a crash (which the idempotency key at least bounds to a narrow window) for guaranteed eventual completion, rather than permanently losing jobs whenever a worker dies mid-task.

stages.prompt: What existing infrastructure supports this pattern rather than building custom dedup logic?
stages.answer: Most modern message queues have deduplication built in for exactly this scenario: Amazon SQS FIFO queues support a MessageDeduplicationId with a 5-minute dedup window natively, and Kafka can achieve similar guarantees via idempotent producers plus consumer-side deduplication using a processed-offsets store. For custom job queues, Redis's atomic SET NX EX (set-if-not-exists with expiry) remains the standard building block underlying most homegrown implementations of this pattern.

diagram:
flowchart LR
    Producer[Job Producer\nmay retry/duplicate] -->|enqueue job| Queue[(Job Queue)]
    Queue --> Worker[Worker]
    Worker -->|SETNX dedup_key TTL| DedupStore[(Redis Dedup Store)]
    DedupStore -->|key existed: skip| Worker
    DedupStore -->|key new: proceed| Process[Process Job]
    Process -->|crash before finish| Expire[Key expires via TTL]
    Expire -.->|reclaimable| Worker
